faqs.title: How do I register as a first-time voter in India?
faqs.answer:
To register as a first-time voter in India, you need to be at least 18 years old and an Indian citizen. The simplest path is online:

1. Visit the **Voter Helpline portal** at [voters.eci.gov.in](https://voters.eci.gov.in) or the **Voter Helpline app** (Android/iOS).
2. Click **'New registration for general electors'** (Form 6).
3. Fill in your personal details, current address, and date of birth.
4. Upload supporting documents: a passport-size photo, age proof (birth certificate / 10th marksheet / passport / Aadhaar) and address proof (Aadhaar / utility bill / passport).
5. Submit. You'll get a reference number to track your application.

Offline: visit your local **Booth Level Officer (BLO)** with the same documents and a printed Form 6.

_Verification typically takes 30–60 days. You'll receive your **EPIC (Voter ID card)** by post or can download an e-EPIC._

faqs.title: What ID do I need to bring to the polling station?
faqs.answer:
On polling day, you primarily need your **Elector Photo Identity Card (EPIC / Voter ID)**. If you don't have your EPIC, the Election Commission of India accepts these alternative photo IDs:

- Aadhaar Card
- Passport
- Driving Licence
- PAN Card
- Service Identity Card (with photograph) issued by Central/State Govt, PSUs, or Public Limited Companies
- Passbook with photograph issued by Bank/Post Office
- MGNREGA Job Card
- Health Insurance Smart Card issued under Ministry of Labour scheme
- Pension document with photograph
- Smart Card issued by RGI under NPR
- Official identity cards issued to MPs/MLAs/MLCs

_Your name **must** be on the electoral roll for that polling booth — bring any one of these, plus check your name in advance on [electoralsearch.eci.gov.in](https://electoralsearch.eci.gov.in)._

faqs.title: How do I find my polling booth?
faqs.answer:
Your assigned polling booth is decided by the Election Commission based on your registered address. To find it:

1. Visit [electoralsearch.eci.gov.in](https://electoralsearch.eci.gov.in)
2. Search by your **EPIC number** OR by your name + state + district.
3. The page shows your part number, serial number, and the **polling booth address**.

Alternatively, the **Voter Helpline app** has a 'Search My Name' feature that returns the same data.

This assistant's _Find polling-style venues_ tool can show you typical venues nearby (schools, halls), but only the official portal can tell you your *assigned* booth — always cross-check there.

faqs.title: What are the polling hours?
faqs.answer:
General Lok Sabha and State Assembly elections in India typically run from **7:00 AM to 6:00 PM** local time. Specific timings vary slightly by:

- **Constituency** — some Naxal-affected or remote areas close earlier (e.g. 4 PM).
- **Special booths** — model polling stations may have specific timings.

If you arrive at the polling station **before 6 PM** (or the official close time), you are entitled to vote even if you wait in queue past closing time. The Election Commission issues queue tokens to those present at closing.

_Check exact timings on your state CEO's website or in the press release for that election day._

faqs.title: How do EVMs and VVPAT work?
faqs.answer:
**EVM (Electronic Voting Machine)** has two units:

- **Ballot Unit** — has buttons next to each candidate's name + symbol; the voter presses one.
- **Control Unit** — held by the Polling Officer; releases one ballot at a time and stores votes.

**VVPAT (Voter-Verifiable Paper Audit Trail)** is a small printer attached to the ballot unit. After you press the button:

1. A paper slip prints showing the candidate's serial number, name, and party symbol.
2. The slip is visible through a glass window for **7 seconds**.
3. Then it's automatically cut and dropped into a sealed VVPAT box.

VVPAT slips from a randomly selected polling station **per assembly segment** are physically counted and matched against EVM totals to verify accuracy.

EVMs are **not connected** to the internet, Bluetooth, or any external network — they're standalone, sealed devices.

faqs.title: Who can vote via postal ballot?
faqs.answer:
Postal ballot is available to specific categories of voters who can't be physically present at a polling booth:

- **Service voters**: armed forces, paramilitary, government employees on election duty, and those posted abroad.
- **Special voters**: people on preventive detention.
- **Persons with Disabilities (PwD)** and **senior citizens above 85 years** — can opt for postal ballot via Form 12-D.
- **COVID-19 / essential service workers** — applied during specific elections.
- **Voters on essential services** notified by the ECI for that election.

Apply via **Form 12 / 12-A / 12-D** depending on category, well before the election. Filled ballots must reach the Returning Officer by the time counting starts.

faqs.title: What is NOTA?
faqs.answer:
**NOTA (None Of The Above)** is the last option on every EVM ballot. Pressing it records that you voted but didn't support any candidate.

- NOTA was introduced by the Supreme Court in 2013.
- Currently, NOTA votes are **counted but don't affect the result** — even if NOTA gets more votes than every candidate, the candidate with the most non-NOTA votes still wins.
- It exists to give voters a way to register a formal protest while still participating in the democratic process.

If you choose NOTA, your secrecy is preserved — the EVM doesn't link your choice to your identity.

faqs.title: What is the Election Commission of India and what does it do?
faqs.answer:
The **Election Commission of India (ECI)** is an autonomous constitutional authority established under Article 324 of the Constitution. It conducts elections to:

- **Lok Sabha** (lower house of Parliament)
- **Rajya Sabha** (upper house of Parliament)
- **State Legislative Assemblies** (Vidhan Sabhas)
- **State Legislative Councils** (Vidhan Parishads)
- **Office of the President** and **Vice-President**

It does **not** conduct local body elections (Panchayat, Municipality) — those are run by State Election Commissions.

The ECI:
- Maintains the electoral roll
- Issues Voter ID cards (EPIC)
- Decides election schedules
- Enforces the Model Code of Conduct
- Recognises political parties and allots symbols
- Supervises EVMs/VVPAT

It operates from **Nirvachan Sadan, New Delhi**, with a Chief Election Commissioner and two Election Commissioners. Official site: [eci.gov.in](https://eci.gov.in).

faqs.title: What is the Model Code of Conduct?
faqs.answer:
The **Model Code of Conduct (MCC)** is a set of guidelines issued by the Election Commission to govern the behaviour of political parties, candidates, and the government during elections.

It comes into force from the **date elections are announced** and stays until results are declared.

Key rules:
- No new policy announcements, transfers of officials, or use of public funds for political gain.
- No religion / caste-based appeals.
- No personal attacks unrelated to public conduct.
- Campaign restrictions: silence period 48 hours before polling.
- Vehicle / loudspeaker permits required.

The MCC is **not legally enforceable**, but the ECI can publicly censure violators and refer cases to law enforcement for related offences (bribery, hate speech, etc.).

faqs.title: How are votes counted?
faqs.answer:
Counting happens at **designated counting centres**, usually a few weeks after polling, on a single 'counting day' announced by the ECI.

**Process:**
1. **Postal ballots** are counted first (usually starting at 8 AM).
2. **EVM votes** are counted next, by polling station (each round counts 14 EVMs).
3. After every round, results are announced and uploaded to the ECI's results portal.
4. **VVPAT verification**: 5 randomly selected polling stations per assembly segment have their VVPAT slips physically counted and matched.
5. The **Returning Officer** declares the winner once all rounds + VVPAT match.

Observers from each candidate can be present. The whole process is video-recorded.

Live results: [results.eci.gov.in](https://results.eci.gov.in) on counting day.
